coredata.dc:title: Molybdenum disulfide-based materials with enzyme-like characteristics for biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiangang
Cai, Ru
Zhang, Yunguang
Mu, Xiaoyu
coredata.dc:description: Nanozyme, a kind of nanomaterials with enzymatic activity, has been developing vigorously over the past years owing to its advantages such as low-cost, easy storage, ease of use in harsh environments and so on, compared with natural enzymes. At present, as a typical two-dimensional nanomaterial, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and their hybrids with unexpected enzyme-like activities have caused wide attention. In this review, we mainly investigated the enzyme-like activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials, including peroxidase-like activity, catalase-like activity and superoxide dismutase-like activity. Furthermore, we systematically introduce recent research progress of MoS2 based nanomaterials in the fields of biological applications such as radiation protection, cancer therapy, antibacterial, and wound healing. Finally, the current challenges and perspectives of MoS2 based nanomaterials in the future are also discussed and proposed. We expect this review may be signiﬁcant to understand the properties of MoS2 based nanomaterials and the development of two-dimensional nanomaterials with enzyme mimicking activities.

head.ce:title: Molybdenum disulfide-based materials with enzyme-like characteristics for biological applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozyme, a kind of nanomaterials with enzymatic activity, has been developing vigorously over the past years owing to its advantages such as low-cost, easy storage, ease of use in harsh environments and so on, compared with natural enzymes. At present, as a typical two-dimensional nanomaterial, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and their hybrids with unexpected enzyme-like activities have caused wide attention. In this review, we mainly investigated the enzyme-like activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials, including peroxidase-like activity, catalase-like activity and superoxide dismutase-like activity. Furthermore, we systematically introduce recent research progress of MoS2 based nanomaterials in the fields of biological applications such as radiation protection, cancer therapy, antibacterial, and wound healing. Finally, the current challenges and perspectives of MoS2 based nanomaterials in the future are also discussed and proposed. We expect this review may be signiﬁcant to understand the properties of MoS2 based nanomaterials and the development of two-dimensional nanomaterials with enzyme mimicking activities.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that researchers obtained graphene through mechanical exfoliation in 2004, since then, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have aroused great interest among scientists and have become a research hotspot for many years [1]. As an emerging class of nanomaterials, till today, 2D nanomaterials are not limited to graphene and graphene-based nanomaterials, but also include transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), antimonene [2], MXene nanosheets, black phosphorus (BP), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and so on [3–9]. 2D nanomaterials possess the feature of dimensional confinement and display unique chemical, physical, electronic, photonic and catalytic properties. To date, 2D nanomaterials have been used in many fields [10], such as electrocatalysis [11], photocatalysis [12], sensors [13], electrochemical energy storage [14], and biomedicine [1,15].

In particular, 2D-TMDs have been widely used in therapeutics, bioimaging and biosensing [16,17]. These stoichiometry MX2 are made up of hexagonal layers of metal atoms (M) sandwiched between two layers of chalcogen atoms (X) [18]. As a typical 2D-TMDs, MoS2 is constituted by S-Mo-S layers combined together by van der Waals force [19]. At the present, the preparation methods of MoS2 nanostructure are mainly divided into top-down and bottom-up methods [20]. Top-down methods include mechanical exfoliation [21,22], liquid phase exfoliation [23,24], chemical exfoliation [25], and electrochemical exfoliation [26,27]. Bottom-up methods consist of chemical vapor deposition [28,29] and hydrothermal method [30–32]. The bulk MoS2 has a special band structure different from graphene, that is, indirect band gap of 1.2 eV. and the band gap increases with the decrease of the number of MoS2 layers. The monolayer MoS2 as a semiconductor with the direct bandgap of 1.8 eV [33], shows unique electronic and optical properties, and has great potential applications in catalysts, transistors, photodetector, biosensing, and solar cells [34–36]. The layered MoS2 also owns splendid mechanical performance, tribological performance and lubrication property and can be used as the lubricants [37]. In addition, MoS2 possesses large surface specific area, excellent chemical stability and high electronic conductivity, which is helpful for the applications of electrochemical biosensors [20,38–41], batteries [42], supercapacitors [43,44] etc.. Although scientists have made many achievements in the applications of MoS2, it should be clear that the research on MoS2 is not enough and needs further exploration [45].

Natural enzymes as biocatalysts play a critical role in living systems, mainly composed of proteins or catalytic RNA molecules [46]. The advantages of natural enzymes include high activity, good biocompatibility, high substrate selectivity and so on. However, there are still some problems that limit the application and the development of enzymes, such as poor stability, difficulty in storage, hard to use in harsh environments and so on [47,48]. Nowadays, a lot of nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity have been found [49–60]. The concept of artificial enzymes was proposed by Ronald Breslow for enzyme mimics [55]. So far, many scientists have devoted themselves to the research of nanozyme, and many reviews have summarized and prospected the researches of this field recently [61–69]. Researches showed that MoS2 as well as different types of MoS2-based nanomaterials possessed peroxidase-like activity (POD-like activity), catalase-like activity (CAT-like activity) and superoxide dismutase-like activities (SOD-like activity). Meanwhile, MoS2 exhibits benefits such as facile preparation, low cost, relatively low toxicity, satisfactory biodegradability and rapid excretion [70–72]. In this review, we mainly talk about the enzyme-like activities of MoS2, MoS2/carbon based, MoS2/metal based and MoS2/metal oxide based nanomaterials. Besides, the biological applications of MoS2 such as cancer treatment, radioprotection, and antibiosis are also introduced.

POD have the ability to catalyze the substrate oxidation. As is known, Gao et al. first found magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles featured with intrinsic POD-like activity [73]. At present, nanomaterials with POD-like activity have got increasing research attention. Nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [74], MOF [75–77], MoSe2 [78], WS2 [79], WSe2 [80], N-rGO [81], O-CNTs [82] and FeS2 [83] were convinced to possess POD-like property. Lin et al. found that MoS2 nanosheets owned intrinsic POD-like catalytic activity at first [84]. Selecting TMB and H2O2 as the substrates, TMB is adsorbed on the surface of MoS2 nanosheets and provides lone-pair electrons to MoS2 nanosheets from the amino groups, which might relate to the P-type properties of the less layered MoS2, and increase the electron density and mobility of MoS2 nanosheet. This could promote the electron transfer from MoS2 nanosheets to H2O2 and catalyze H2O2 to produce H2O and •OH in acidic media, and oxidize TMB to generate a blue product. The POD-like catalytic activity of MoS2 nanosheets were explored by steady-state kinetics, and the catalytic mechanism of MoS2 nanosheets might follow a ping-pong reaction mechanism. MoS2 nanosheets bind to one substrate and release the first product, then bind to another substrate and release the second product, which indicates that MoS2 nanosheets have similar behavior to biological enzyme in molecular level and have intrinsic enzyme-like catalytic activity. Chen et al. discovered that MoS2 nanosheets possessed POD-, CAT-, SOD-like activities [85]. The catalytic mechanism of MoS2 nanosheets with POD-like activities can be expressed as the following steps [55,86]:
(1)
M
o 6+
+TMB+2O
H -
→ M
o 4+
+ox-TMB+2
H 2

(2)

First, TMB is adsorbed on the surface of MoS2 nanosheets, donating lone-pair electrons to convert Mo6+ into Mo4+. Second, the MoS2 nanosheets transfer electrons to H2O2 molecules, converting Mo4+ to Mo6+ and enhance the electron transfer between MoS2 nanosheets and TMB. At last, a blue product yields and H2O2 is reduced to OH−. That is to say, the established molecular mechanism proved that the POD-like activity of MoS2 nanosheets stems from its ability to promote electron transfer between TMB and H2O2 instead of producing •OH.
Recently, MoS2 based nanomaterials were fabricated by different methods to improve its POD-like catalytic activity as summarized in Table 1 .

Scientists are committed to enhancing the POD-like catalytic activity, catalysis velocity, and substrate affinity of MoS2. MoS2 NSs are endowed with distinct large specific surface areas and abundant active edges, and the catalytic activity can be enhanced by integrating with other nanomaterials [100]. Size and dispersibility could influence the catalytic activity of nanomaterials. For instance, according to a simple hydrothermal method, Yu et al. reported that polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) functionalized MoS2 with the properties of ultra-small size, enhanced catalytic activity and good biocompatibility [89]. Zhao et al. also fabricated PEGylated MoS2 (PEG-MoS2) with low toxicity and strong practicality. The POD-like catalytic activity of PEG-MoS2 was enhanced compared with MoS2 nanosheets, which can be used for H2O2 detection [90]. Besides, using a one-pot reaction, Lei et al. designed an algae-like MoS2/Polypyrrole (MoS2/PPy) nanostructure. The POD-like activity of MoS2/PPy was enhanced compared with pure MoS2 nanoflowers, pure PPy, and their physical mixtures [88], which mainly due to the synergistic coupling effect of MoS2 and PPy. Therefore, surface modification is a good way to improve the stability and the POD-like activity of MoS2. According to a one-pot hydrothermal method, Yu et al. fabricated MoS2 nanoflakes (MoS2 NFs) with intrinsic POD-like activity. The NFs exhibited good dispersibility when modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI), polyacrylic acid (PAA), PVP or cysteine (Cys). MoS2 NFs modified by Cys performed the best POD-like catalytic activity [101]. For the Cys-MoS2 NFs/ABTS system, a new catalytic reaction mechanism different from the Cys-MoS2 NFs/TMB system was put forward via steady-state kinetics method. They presumed that Cys-MoS2-ABTS-H2O2 transient state could form at first via a rapid ordered random mechanism [102], ABTS exchanged charges with H2O2 via Cys as an electronic transfer bridge for redox couples (ABTS and H2O2) on this basis, and •OH was generated finally. The catalysis reaction was ascribed to the role of the charge transfer bridge of H2O2 and ABTS (Fig. 1 A). It was proved that Cys-MoS2 NFs behaved the best POD-like activity with TMB or ABTS as substrate (Fig. 1B), which could be used for H2O2 and glucose detection. The group of TA + Cys-MoS2+H2O2 could generate more •OH compared with others (Fig. 1C). Thus, this work provides useful information for future research on enzyme-like mechanisms.

Besides, the formation of heterogeneous structures helped to improve the catalytic activity of the material, which can be realized by heteroatom doping as reported by Zhang et al. They fabricated hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and nitride doped molybdenum disulfide (N-MoS2) nano-composites (h-BN/N-MoS2), which can be used for H2O2 detection [92]. Consequently, inspired by the method of heteroatom doping [104], Feng et al. designed N-doped MoS2 with the help of the formed Mo-N bounds using a controllable N2 plasma treatment method, which owned a lower Fermi level than MoS2 based on density functional theory (DFT) and with the increased electron transferring [103]. The fabricated N-doped MoS2 possessed enhanced POD-like activity compared with MoS2 nanosheets, and could catalyze H2O2 and oxidize TMB evidently (Fig. 1D, E). Kinetic studies certified that treated with plasma for two minutes, the POD-like activity of N-doped MoS2 was greatly improved, over 3-fold higher than MoS2. The mechanism of enhanced POD-like activity of N-doped MoS2 could be also evaluated by terephthalic acid (TA) in the TMB-H2O2 system in which H2O2 can produce more •OH with the help of N-doped MoS2 (Fig. 1F).

Experiments showed that the catalysis mechanism of MoS2-based nanomaterials might follow ping-pong reaction mechanism. The POD-like activity of MoS2 might derive from the conversion of Mo6+ and Mo4+. As mentioned above, we find that size and dispersibility are important factors to influence the POD-like activity of MoS2-based nanomaterials. It is also proved that the surface modification, the heterogeneous structure formation and the integration of MoS2 with other materials are effective ways to improve the POD-like activity of MoS2-based nanomaterials. The potential mechanism of enhanced POD-like activity could be ascribed to the lower Fermi level and the accelerated electron transfer rate of the prepared MoS2-based nanomaterials.

CAT could catalyze H2O2 into H2O and O2 [105]. There are a great number of nanomaterials showing CAT-like activity such as MnO2@PtCo nanocatalyst [106], CeO2 nanoparticles [107,108], PB [86], Co3O4 [109], and PDMS-AuNPs [110]. As reported by Chen et al., MoS2 nanosheets possessed CAT-like activity [85]. The catalytic mechanism of MoS2 nanosheets with CAT-like activity can be expressed as the following steps:
(3)

H 2

+2O

→ M
o 4+

(4)

+2O

Thus, MoS2 could accelerate the decomposition of H2O2. Inspired by the result that ROS can be eliminated by MoS2 NPs [111], Ren et al. designed (3-carboxypropyl) triphenyl-phosphonium bromide-conjugated 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[amino (polyethylene glycol)-2000]-functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (TPP-MoS2 QDs) which could target mitochondria (Fig. 2
A) [112]. TPP-MoS2 possessed with SOD, CAT-like activity, the CAT-like activity of TPP-MoS2 were shown in Fig. 2 B-D.

By combining ROS scavenging ability, down-regulating the pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, upregulating TGF-β, TPP-MoS2 can realize the protection of neurons and change the inflammatory M1 phenotype to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. Thus, they proposed a novel way to mitigate Alzheimer's disease (AD). Furthermore, Chen et al. confirmed that the fullerene-like MoS2 (F-MoS2) nanoparticles can be used as efficient lubricants and antioxidants for HA-based artificial synovial fluid [113]. The formation of F-MoS2 nanoparticles is revealed in Fig. 2 E. With the CAT-like activity, F-MoS2 could convert H2O2 into O2, the higher concentration of O2, the lower ESR peak intensity of CTPO, as shown in Fig. 2 F. It can be concluded that F-MoS2 nanoparticles can be used for osteoarthritis (OA) therapy and oxidative stress in the future.

It can be seen that there are few reports on the CAT-like activity of MoS2-based nanomaterials. The CAT-like activity of MoS2 nanosheets might due to the conversion of Mo6+ and Mo4+ based on the current researches. Studies have shown that MoS2 could eliminate ROS and can be used for diseases therapy such as Alzheimer's and osteoarthritis, which revealed promising medical applications.

SOD could exterminate the superoxide radicals (O2 •-) by transforming O2 •- into H2O2 and O2 [69]. Many materials were proved to possess SOD-like activity such as Mn3O4 [114] and HPBZs [115]. MoS2 nanosheets possessed SOD-like activity as reported by Chen et al. [85]. The catalytic mechanism of MoS2 nanosheets with SOD-like activities can be described as the following steps:
(5)

o 6+

O 2
(6)
M

O 2

→ M

+
O 2
+
H +

F-MoS2 nanoparticles are also featured with SOD-like activity [113]. The SOD-like activity of F-MoS2 was determined in three ways including Py autoxidation, UV/vis assay and ESR spectroscopy (Fig. 3
A-C). The electron transfer between the Mo4+ on the surface of F-MoS2 nanoparticles and O2 •– could be enhanced owing to the electrostatic attraction. It can be presumed that the SOD-like activity of F-MoS2 nanoparticles originates from the conversion between the oxidized state Mo6+ and the reduced state Mo4+ [116]. With the properties of SOD-, CAT-like activities, a cascade catalytic system can be formed, which could finally convert O2 •– to O2. When F-MoS2 and hyaluronic acid (HA) are combined, the contents of ROS in artificial synovial fluid involving HA can be regulated.

MoS2 nanosheets possessed SOD-, CAT-, POD-like activities under physiological conditions as we described in the previous sections (Fig. 3 D) [85]. Such results have a biochemical basis, it was reported that the active site of the enzyme contains molybdenum which is widely distributed in most organisms [117]. MoS2 nanosheets can be used to form an antioxidant system. Its SOD-like activity could accelerate the conversion of O2 •– to H2O2, CAT- and POD-like activities catalyze H2O2 into O2 and H2O subsequently as well as the enzymatic antioxidant system. Therefore, the fabricated MoS2 nanosheets exhibit an attractive antioxidant prospect in the pathological processes. Recently, Wang et al. reported that the vertical heterostructure of MoS2/rGO owned OXD(oxidase)-, POD-,CAT-like activities, and this work provides new ideas for the design of antibacterial materials by integrating defect chemistry, nanotopology and catalytic properties [118].

Obviously, there are few reports on the SOD-like activity of MoS2. The SOD-like activity of MoS2 can be measured by three ways, that is, Py autoxidation, UV/vis assay and ESR spectroscopy. It is speculated that the conversion of Mo6+/Mo4+ is an important source of the SOD-like activity of MoS2 nanosheets.

In general, a lot of reports focused on the POD-like activity of MoS2-based nanomaterials currently, while its CAT- and SOD-like activities are less reported. It can be concluded that size, dispersibility, morphology and valence could affect the catalytic activities of MoS2-based nanomaterials. Surface modification, combination with other materials, heteroatom doping, and formation of the heterogeneous structures can be used to increase catalytic activities of the nanomaterials. However, the active sites of MoS2-based nanomaterials and their catalytic mechanism are not yet exact and need to be further explored.

Carbon-based nanomaterials include carbon nanodots [119], carbon quantum dot [120–122], graphene oxide (GO) [123–125], graphene [126–128], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [82], g-C3N4 [129] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [125]. Researchers are committed to elevating the catalytic efficiency of enzymes. Hassanzadeh et al. reported that the mixture of MoS2 QDs and GQDs displayed a synergetic POD-like activity, the enhanced synergetic effect can be ascribed to the electronic interaction of the two components [130]. N-doped carbon nanotubes (NCNTs@MoS2) hybrid composites were also owned with improved POD-like activity [131]. Using the method of protonation-assisted ion exchange, Ju et al. designed MoS2 QDs functionalized g-C3N4 nanosheets (MoS2@CNNS). The POD-like activity was improved nearly 4 times compared with every single one, which can be attributed to the synergistic effects and the following high conductivity as well as electron-transfer capability [94]. Furthermore, according to a one-pot bottom-up strategy, Liu et al. developed MoS2/g-C3N4 heterojuncted nanosheets (MoS2/g-C3N4 HNs) [132]. The acquired nanozyme with heterojunction sites exhibited enhanced POD-like activity (Fig. 4 A) for three reasons, the accelerated electron transfer rate, the synergistic effect of components to produce more radicals and high affinity to TMB and H2O2. The POD-like activity of MoS2/g-C3N4 HNs was measured by ABTS, TMB and o-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride (OPD) (Fig. 4B). The changed absorption and color suggested that this nanozyme could be used as a mimic enzyme for POD with promising potential. The catalytic mechanism can be depicted as the increase of •OH radical from H2O2 after promoting the electron transfer between MoS2/g-C3N4 and H2O2, thus improving the oxidation reaction rate of TMB. The POD-like activity of MoS2/g-C3N4 HNs was better than MoS2, g-C3N4 and their physic mixture, which ascribed to the synergetic effect of the two elements. This work provides useful information for promoting the POD-like activity of 2D nanozyme.

Peng et al. fabricated MoS2/GO hybrids that displayed a higher intrinsic POD-like activity compared with HRP as tested by TMB kits. They discovered that visible and near-infrared lights irradiation were good ways to strengthen the POD-like activity of hybrids [133]. It was proved that MoS2/GO-modified electrode behaved the best catalytic activity to H2O2 compared with MoS2 and GO. The rapid electron transfer, high conductivity and the synergistic effect of the two parts are the important reasons for the enhancement of the catalytic activity of MoS2/GO. The glucose content in serum could be measured by MoS2/GO (Fig. 4C). Under infrared radiation, the catalytic reactions of different catalysts were improved (Fig. 4D), which could boost the amount of H2O2 oxidation compared with the reaction without light. We believe that the designed MoS2/GO mimetic has extensive application prospects on medical diagnostics and photocatalysis.

Metal-based nanomaterials such as Ag@Au [134], Pt NCs [135], ZnO-Pd [136], Cu-C [137] have been reported with enzyme-like properties. Nirala et al. fabricated AuNPs@MoS2 quantum dots with improved POD-like activity [97]. The prepared composite followed the enzyme kinetics, which could realize glucose detect in a buffer solution with a convincing result. According to ascorbic acid reduction, AuNPs were modified on a MoS2/carbon surface termed as MoS2/C-Au600 as fabricated by Sun et al. The nanocomposite behaved POD-like activity and followed Michaelis-Menten enzymatic kinetics as well as ping-pong mechanisms, which could be used for visualizing cancer cells via colorimetric immunoassays [93]. Besides, Nirala et al. designed MoS2 NRs-AuNPs which possessed synergistically enhanced POD-like catalytic activity (Fig. 4E) and have the ability to test free cholesterol in human serum with high sensitivity [138]. The essential of TMB color reaction is the presence of catalyst. The catalyst can accept the electrons from the non-bonding orbital of TMB and owned enough Fermi energy to transfer the electrons to the lowest unoccupied molecule orbits (LUMO) of H2O2 via the implementation of the redox potential of H2O2 (0.38 eV) [139]. It was reported that the POD-like catalytic activity of MoS2 NRs may be due to its P-type properties (the lack of sulfur) [140]. The system of TMB + MoS2 NRs-AuNPs+H2O2 showed the highest absorption, accompanied by the intense blue shade (Fig. 4F). This might due to the excessive number of electrons in the Au NPs conduction band, which are in turn provided to the underlying p-type MoS2 NRs matrix. As a result, fermi levels rise much higher when receiving electrons from TMB. TMB transfers its electrons to the catalyst at λmax = 285 nm, and transfers the electrons to H2O2 for its reduction, which could occur through free radicals or ionic mechanisms [139]. It was proved that H2O2 reduction followed the free radical mechanism in this work. Finally, the positively charged TMB was stabilized with the parent TMB molecule, which gave rise to the charged transfer chromophore of TMB with characteristic peaks (located at 370, 652 nm), produced blue shades. Thus, through the study of the absorption, it was proved that the synergistic effect enhanced the enzyme-like activity of MoS2 NRs-AuNPs, and the system of MoS2 NRs-AuNPs-H2O2-TMB were essential to detect H2O2. This work provides a novel approach to build new composites and is beneficial to biomimetics.

Hassanzadeh et al. not only designed AgNC/MoS2-NS nanocomposites with great POD-like activity but also fabricated ChOx-MOF with excellent catalytic activity, and the composed systems could be used to detect cholesterol in human blood samples [141]. The obtained ChOx-MOF could oxidize cholesterol to yield H2O2, and could be further applied to oxidize terephthalic acid with the addition of AgNC/MoS2-NS. The POD-like activity of AgNC/MoS2-NS was examined by TA-H2O2 and electrochemical experiments, the synergetic effects of AgNC and MoS2-NS propelled the catalytic activity to a great extent. In short, this work provides a valuable reference for subsequent cholesterol detection and showed broad prospects in the field of biomedicine.

Bimetallic nanoparticles can greatly improve the catalytic activity of nanomaterials. By anchoring FePt NPs onto few-layer molybdenum disulfide nanosheets (F-MoS2 NSs), Hu et al. constructed F-MoS2-FePt NCs nanocomposite with enhanced POD-like activity, which can be used for H2O2 detection [96]. Tan et al. designed an immunosensor based on PtPd NCs@MoS2 enzymes, possessing enhanced POD-like activity which may due to the distinct nano-cube stacking structure as well as the large specific surface area of MoS2, and can be used for Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBs Ag) detection based on its excellent conductivity and high specific surface area [142]. Compared with other groups (Fig. 4G), the reaction system contained PtPd NCs@MoS2 behaved the darkest color and the largest absorbance, which manifested the better catalytic performance. The double reciprocal curve (Fig. 4H) and the Michaelis-Menten equation were utilized for calculating kinetic constants and catalytic efficiency of various nanozymes, and the catalytic reaction of PtPd NCs@MoS2 followed the Michaelis-Menten kinetic equation. TA was selected to capture •OH. The fluorescence intensity of PtPd NCs@MoS2 was greater than MoS2 and PtPdNCs, which confirmed the enhanced POD properties and the •OH mechanism of PtPd NCs@MoS2. It was proved that the electrochemical immunosensor based on PtPd NCs@MoS2 exhibited promising potential in HBs Ag detection owing to its reproducibility, stability, which could be beneficial to discern cancer cells.

Similar to carbon and metal-based nanometer materials mentioned above, metal oxides-based nanomaterials also have intrinsic enzyme-like properties such as cerium oxide [143], magnetic nanoparticles [144], copper oxide [145], and vanadium oxides [146]. Nandwana et al. fabricated Fe3O4/MoS2 possessed with higher POD-like activity. Glucose test strips based on Fe3O4/MoS2 could visually distinguish the glucose concentrations of healthy and diabetic, which showed the great prospect of noninvasive point-of-care medical diagnostics [99]. By dispersing MgFe2O4 into a hydrothermal system, Zhang et al. fabricated MoS2@MgFe2O4 nanocomposite with enhanced POD-mimicking activity compared with its two components, which could be further applied for glucose detection [147]. MoS2@MgFe2O4 behaved the deeper color reaction and the maximum absorption intensity (Fig. 4I), when positively charged TMB molecules were in contact with MoS2@MgFe2O4, lone-pair electrons were provided to MoS2@MgFe2O4 from the two amino groups of TMB and promoted the catalytic activity. According to a facile solvothermal method, Liu et al. fabricated 3D magnetic MoS2/Fe3O4 nanocomposites with enhanced POD-like catalytic activity and can be utilized for perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) detection [148]. During the oxidation process of TMB, MoS2/Fe3O4 behaved the best catalytic activity (Fig. 4J). Mott-schottky curves proved that decoration by Fe3O4 could reduce the Fermi energy level of the catalyst and facilitate electron transfer to advance the oxidation of TMB. which can be ascribed to the facilitated electron transfer.

Utilizing electrospinning, calcination and hydrothermal, Zhu et al. designed TiO2/MoS2 core-shell hybrid nanofibers [149] with POD-like activity (Fig. 4K). On account of the synergistic effect, TiO2/MoS2 showed enhanced catalytic activity compared with TiO2 nanofibers and MoS2 nanosheets alone (Fig. 4L). With the medium thickness of the MoS2 shell, TiO2/MoS2-2 displayed the highest POD-like activity. This work provides useful information for colorimetric sensing, medical diagnosis, etc.

From the above studies, we discovered that the enzyme-like activity of MoS2 nanomaterials could be enhanced by combination with carbon-based, metal-based, metal-oxide based nanomaterials due to the synergistic effects and the accelerated electron-transfer capability.

In summary, various of MoS2 based nanomaterials have been reported with enzyme-like properties, especially POD-like activity. It is deduced that the enzyme-like activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials were derived from the conversion of Mo6+/Mo4+ redox couple. The catalysis mechanism of MoS2 based nanomaterials may follow ping-pong reaction mechanism. Furthermore, the structure including sizes, morphology, surface modifications, doping different elements and heterogeneous structures, plays an important role in the enzyme-like activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials. However, the systematic steady-state kinetics experiments and detailed theoretical calculations of reaction path still need to be further explored to evaluate quantitatively and understand the mechanism of enzyme-like activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials. Moreover, MoS2 based nanomaterials with POD-like activity can be used for detecting glucose in buffer solution and serum, detecting cholesterol in human serum, detecting perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), detecting Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBs Ag) and observing cancer cells by colorimetric immunoassays. Whereas, it is necessary to further explore the application of MoS2 based nanomaterials in the biomedical field, and to combine various disciplines to achieve a deeper understanding of MoS2 based nanomaterials.

Ionizing radiations as a kind of electromagnetic radiation have greatly promoted the progress of modern medical science, which is involved in a great number of clinical treatment methods, for instance, radiotherapy (RT) [150,151], photodynamic therapy (PDT), positron emission tomography (PET) [152]. High-energy radiation (X-ray, γ-ray) can elicit cell apoptosis via interaction with DNA molecules or H2O to form ROS, RT is the main method of clinical treatment of cancer at present [153]. However, ionizing radiation may have adverse effects on normal tissues in the process of tumor-killing, causing the serious toxic effect on the human body [154], thus the ideal radiotherapy effect is difficult to obtain. Therefore, it is an urgency to develop radioprotectants to prevent normal tissues from radiation injuries.

In recent years, the development of nanomaterials and nanobiotechnology has provided broad opportunities for tumor radiosensitization. It was proved that various nanometer materials can be used as common nanoradioprotectors [155], such as CeO2 nanoparticles [156], Mn clusters [157], C60 [158], Bi2S3-MoS2 [159], PEGylation-MIL-101(Fe) [160]. Zhang et al. devised cysteine-protected MoS2 dots endowed with the high catalytic activity which could be used as radioprotectants (Fig. 5
A) [161]. The survival rates of mice reflect the radiation protection effect of cysteine-protected MoS2 dots directly. C57BL/6 mice were selected as the model of radiation protection experimental in vivo. After 15 days, the survival rate of mice dropped to zero in the presence of high-energy radiation (137Cs, 3600 Ci, 7.5 Gy), whereas, the survival rate of mice increased to 7.1, 42.9 and 78.6 % when the doses of cysteine-protected MoS2 dots (200 μL, 5 mg/mL) were 10, 20 and 50 mg/kg respectively (Fig. 5B). By exploring the effect of the external radiation of cysteine, it was proved that the radiation protection effect stemmed from MoS2.

In this work, compared with untreated cells, the survival rates of A31 cells treated with cysteine-protected MoS2 dots were escalated with the enhanced gamma-ray, which represented the radioprotective effect of cysteine-protected MoS2 dots for cells in vitro as showed in Fig. 5 C. DNA damage caused by radiation were also explored, based on the survey of the tail moment, demonstrating that cysteine-protected MoS2 dots have a protective effect on cells irradiated by gamma-ray (Fig. 5D).

Furthermore, total DNA from bone marrow cells is an important factor for the evaluation of ionizing radiation, and cysteine-protected MoS2 dots can validly reduce the lesion to DNA caused by high-energy radiation. In terms of healthy mice and cysteine-protected MoS2 dots treated mice, the value of optical density (OD) of total DNA dropped from 4.42 to 0.22 and 0.29 respectively in the presence of radiation just one day later (Fig. 5E). Whereas, the value of OD rose to 0.25 and 0.38 a week later. It can be confirmed that cysteine-protected MoS2 dots have the ability to reduce the damage of high-energy radiation to DNA.

Cysteine-protected MoS2 dots could catalyze H2O2 more effectively, even at an injection dose of 50 mg/kg, about 80 % of urine can be excreted by the bladder within 24 hours with no significant toxic effects.

So far, cancer is still the main cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide with no inevitable connection with the development of human society [162]. Liu et al. firstly demonstrated that MoS2 nanosheets functionalized by lipoic acid-modified PEG (LA-PEG) could be used as a drug carrier during the treatment of cancer (Fig. 6
A) [163]. MoS2-PEG loaded with doxorubicin (DOX) showed an excellent synergistic anti-cancer effect. In vivo combination therapy can be performed by intratumoral injection of MoS2-PEG/DOX (Fig. 6B), and the dose of MoS2-PEG/DOX was 10 times higher than local injection. Experimental results showed that the group of MoS2-PEG/DOX could achieve the best therapeutic effect (Fig. 6C). Employing a simple oleum treatment exfoliation method, Yin et al. fabricated chitosan-functionalized MoS2 (MoS2-CS) which possessed with controllable sizes [164]. The prepared MoS2-CS had been developed for near-infrared (NIR) photothermal-triggered drug delivery and could be used to promote cancer therapy, including chemotherapy and photothermal therapy. Furthermore, the Mo-based MoS2-CS could be applied to computed tomography (CT) imaging (Fig. 6D). When loaded with DOX, the response of drug release to 808 nm near NIR laser is controllable. Experimental results showed that MoS2-CS-DOX + NIR could restrain the growth of the tumor more effectively (Fig. 6E). Besides, no significant decrease in the weight of the mice was observed during the experiment, indicating that the treatments had low toxicity in vivo (Fig. 6F). This article provides an important idea for cancer treatment.

Nanozymes with enzyme-like activity can be used for cancer treatment, the combination of more than one nanozymes for the treatment of cancer have attracted widespread attention [165,166]. By putting MoS2 NSs on anisotropic AuNBPs, Maji et al. designed AuNBPs@MoS2 for cancer therapy and two-photon bioimaging [167]. The prepared AuNBPs@MoS2 possessed multiple nanozymetic properties, which could lead to the production of highly toxic ROS, and cause cell death (Fig. 6G). The group of AuNBPs@MoS2-PEG+H2O2+NIR could obtain the maximum therapeutic efficiency with the cell viability of 13.2 % (Fig. 6H). The production of ROS in HeLa cells could be observed by CLSM image (Fig. 6I). thus, cancer cells could be killed effectively by AuNBPs@MoS2-PEG which owned an enhanced therapeutic effect.

Diseases caused by bacteria do harm to people’s health until now, and can induce high morbidity and even mortality [168]. Antimicrobial agents mainly involved antibiotics, metal nanoparticles, metal nanoclusters [169], and polymeric materials [170,171]. Particularly, antibiotics were once considered as an important substance that can effectively inhibit infectious bacteria [172]. To date, it is well known that antibiotic resistance is one of the major challenges worldwide [173]. The design of an alternative antimicrobial agent possessed with high efficiency and high benefits to keep ahead of the bacterial evolution is necessary [169]. Nanomaterials provided with POD-like activities have attracted much attention recently [174], which can be used as antibacterial such as MoS2 sheets [175], Au/g-C3N4 [176], Fe3O4 and Fe2O3 [177], Pd nanocrystals [178], single-atom catalysts (SACs) [179], CeO2 [180].

Inspired by studies that rough surfaces can enhance bacterial adhesion and defects to improve catalytic activity, Cao et al. first proposed an innovative strategy of constructing rough surfaces with exposed defect-rich active edges to promote the binding capacity and POD-like activity of nanozymes contemporaneously [181]. The nanozyme was fabricated by combining MoS2 and Cu NWs (R-CMs), which was equipped with bacterial capture and resultful antibacterial characteristics (Fig. 7 A). The enhanced antibacterial effects were mainly caused by two factors: the increased affinity of RCM to the wall of the bacterial cell as well as •OH content. Researches showed that the POD-like activity of nanozymes could be enhanced by photothermal slightly. After NIR light irradiation, S. aureus could be destroyed (Fig. 7 B-D). Therefore, in vitro experiments showed that the designed nanozyme could bind closely with the microbe and exhibited improved antibacterial activity. Undoubtedly, this strategy will provide the basis for the development of alternative antibiotics.

Based on PEG-MoS2 NFs, Yin et al. fabricated a biocompatible antibacterial system possessed with POD catalytic activity and photothermal therapy (PTT) efficacy [71]. They introduced a mechanism for exogenous •OH-enhanced PTT against bacteria. Bacteria could capture PEG-MoS2, and the combination could convert H2O2 into •OH to against bacteria more effectively. The interaction of •OH and bacteria could destroy cell walls and the integrity of the membrane, and 808 nm laser could bring about hyperthermia and promote the antibiotic effect (Fig. 7E). In the existence of H2O2, using the plate count method, they tested the antibacterial ability of PEG-MoS2 against Ampr E. coli and B. subtilis. Particularly, with the addition of 808 nm laser radiated bacteria for 10 min, the bacteria inactivation percentage achieved 100 % for B. subtilis (Fig. 7F, G). All in all, the synergistic antibacterial system provided a better killing outcome for Ampr E. coli and B. subtilis in vitro than catalytic treatment or PTT alone. Furthermore, accelerated GSH oxidation could be caused by 808 nm irradiation, which was more likely to cause bacterial death. In short, this research creates an efficient method for wound disinfection and provides valuable information for biomedical research in the future.

As mentioned above, nanozymes with catalytic characteristics could yield ROS and can be used as antibacterial. Qu and coworkers fabricated MoS2-hydrogel with enhanced antibacterial ability, which could achieve a synergistic bactericidal effect when combined with near-infrared irradiation. Bacteria could be captured and confined by MoS2-hydrogel within the scope of ROS damage through electrostatic interaction (Fig. 7H), which could enhance the antibacterial effect of •OH effectively [182]. An in vitro experiment was carried out to study the antibacterial ability of MoS2-hydrogel resist S. aureus and E. coli. As for E. coli (Fig. 7I), when dealt with MoS2-hydrogel+H2O2, the bacterial vitality decreased obviously compared with other groups. More importantly, it was proved that the synergistic system had the strongest sterilization ability. When treated with MoS2-hydrogel+H2O2+808 nm laser irradiation, the bacteria were seriously damaged. Therefore, the system constructed here supplied a new perspective for the antibacterial research of nanozymes.

Obviously, the unique mechanism of action and effectiveness of nanozymes different from antibiotics makes it an ideal substitute for antibiotics to inhibit drug-resistant bacteria. In fact, nanozymes can be used not only for antibacterial applications but also for wound healing [71,181,182], which could reduce the risk of inflammation and promote wound healing rate by getting rid of the dead cells, and histological analysis confirmed this conclusion.

In summary, MoS2-based nanomaterials exhibited rich biological applications. Firstly, MoS2 dots possessed the radiation protection effect, which due to they can scavenge free radicals. However, long-term biosafety studies of MoS2 need to be strengthened and a uniform standards of radioprotection effectiveness should be developed. Secondly, MoS2-based nanomaterials with high near-infrared (NIR) absorption can be used for drug delivery and cancer treatment. Nevertheless, due to the complexity of cancer treatment, a deeper understanding of nano-biological interactions need to be explored, which in turn promotes its clinical applications. Lastly, MoS2-based nanomaterials can serve as antimicrobial agent due to the POD-like catalytic activity. The antimicrobial activities of nanomaterials at present are mainly focused on Ampr E. coli and B. Subtilis, so it is necessary to further explore the bactericidal effects on other bacteria and broaden their antimicrobial applications.

In recent years, more and more researchers have devoted themselves to the study of MoS2, which can be used in many fields due to their unique physical and chemical properties. This review mainly introduces the enzyme-like catalytic activities as well as the biological applications of MoS2 based nanomaterials. It is proved that MoS2 based nanomaterials possessed with SOD, POD, CAT-like activities. Generally speaking, pH and temperature have important effects on enzyme-like nanomaterials. Up to now, MoS2 based nanomaterials are primarily concentrated on the POD-like activity, while the CAT and SOD-like activities are less reported.

Although nanomaterials based on the enzyme-like properties of MoS2 have made great progress, there are still some challenges and perspectives as follows: (1) The causal sequence between the experimental phenomena and the detailed catalytic mechanisms in the applications of MoS2 based nanomaterials are not clear and should be further comprehended. Moreover, it also requires the cooperation of multidisciplinary and interdisciplinary experts to explore the catalytic mechanism of nanomaterials from different perspectives, to reveal the essential relationship of the structure and catalytic properties, to establish a theoretical system of MoS2 based nanomaterials catalysis, and realize the rational design and precise regulation of MoS2 based nanomaterials finally. (2) Advanced characterization technologies of catalytic nanomaterials like MoS2 need to be further developed to understand the catalytic process more intuitively. (3) The systematic steady-state kinetic analysis and detailed computational research such as DFT need to be further explored to study the catalytic mechanism of MoS2 based nanomaterials. The former is a basic experimental way to evaluate quantitatively the catalytic activities of nanomaterials, the latter helps to understand the relationship between the structure and performance of nanomaterials. Although these nanomaterials exhibit similar enzyme activity, these methods are not sufficient for complex catalytic mechanisms. Consequently, new theoretical methods should be introduced from microscopic perspectives (electrons, atoms, molecules, nanoscales, etc.) to promote the rapid development of this field. (4) The development of MoS2 based nanomaterials with high selectivity and catalytic activity will be the research hotspot in the domain of nanozyme research. It should be realized that the proper surface modifications, aqueous dispersibility, sizes, charges, and reactive sites are important factors for the catalytic activity and specificity of MoS2 based nanomaterials. Furthermore, the catalytic activities can also be improved by heteroatom doping and plasma treatment technology. However, the potential mechanism how these factors affect the catalytic activities of MoS2 based nanomaterials, is poorly understood, which are crucial for the study of the enzyme-like properties of MoS2 based nanomaterials. (5) As mentioned above, MoS2 based nanomaterials behave attractive prospects in the fields of radiation protection, cancer therapy, antibacterial and wound healing. However, pharmacokinetics, biocompatible, excretion, distribution, degradability and cytotoxicity are still considered as the most important factors for the medical applications of MoS2 based nanomaterials, which needs to be better solved. (6) It is necessary to further explore the biomedical applications of MoS2 based nanomaterials for other diseases, and the experimental results are expected to be applied in clinical practices.

Therefore, we should pay more attention to the basic theoretical innovation of MoS2 based nanomaterials in future researches, establish a better theoretical system of nanozyme catalysis, and vigorously promote the industrialization of nanozyme catalysis to apply for more fields.
RC wrote the first draft, XM and YZ modified the manuscript content and format, JX and YZ modified the syntax.
